explanation: The requirement states that whenever prop_0 (the sudden trigger) occurs, this implies that a new inspiration (prop_1) must be initiated, and it must result in prop_2 being true. In LTL, this can be interpreted as: whenever prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 happens and this leads to prop_2. Thus, globally (G), whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must hold, and prop_2 must also hold. That is, G (prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2)).

The statement indicates that whenever a new inspiration (represented by prop_1) is initiated, it must have been set by prop_3 before (i.e., prior to or at the same moment as prop_1). The condition 'drop(PAW) > prop_2' is not an allowable atomic proposition since we can only refer to named atomic propositions, not numerical comparisons. Thus, the LTL translation must ensure that prop_1 is always preceded by (or coincides with) prop_3. This can be captured using: globally, if prop_1 holds, then it must be that prop_3 held sometime earlier or at the same time, i.e., prop_3 must hold at least as early as when prop_1 holds.

The requirement is that whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_2 should also become true (i.e., whenever a new inspiration is initiated by prop_1, prop_2, which acts as a guard for the condition, must also be satisfied at that same point). This is a 'prop_1 implies prop_2' relationship, to hold globally.

The statement says that 'a new inspiration shall be initiated by prop_1'. This means whenever prop_1 becomes true, it signals the start of a new inspiration, which we model in LTL as: Whenever prop_1 is true, it must precede (or is at) the start of a new inspiration. Since the rest of how inspiration progresses is not specified among the atomic propositions, we can directly indicate that initiation is marked by prop_1. Additionally, since it is set by the user (via drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV), but since we cannot refer to such conditions, only prop_1 is used as the initiating signal.

The statement says that a 'new inspiration' is initiated by prop_1, and that prop_1 is set by the user, with the result that prop_2 holds. In LTL, this can be phrased as: Whenever prop_1 becomes true, eventually prop_2 must become true. This is written as G(prop_1 -> F(prop_2)).

The specification requires that PSV mode is characterized by 'prop_0 by prop_1' (ITS_PSV), which is to be set by the user as 'prop_2'. In LTL, this means whenever prop_2 holds (user sets the mode), prop_0 by prop_1 must be sustained, i.e., globally if prop_2 holds, then prop_0 and prop_1 must both hold.

The formula states that globally (always), if prop_1 (initiation) occurs, then eventually, prop_2 will occur and when it does, prop_3 will hold. Here, 'a new inspiration shall be initiated by prop_1' means 'if prop_1', and 'which shall prop_2 (i.e., it yields the condition prop_3)' means 'then prop_2 occurs and at that moment prop_3 holds'.

The sentence says that in PSV mode, a new inspiration is initiated by prop_1, which is set by the user and results in prop_3 being true. Focusing on this transition, the formula should express that whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_3 follows, i.e., triggering prop_1 causes prop_3. In LTL, this is commonly captured as: globally, if prop_1 happens then eventually prop_3 will be true.

The natural language statement describes that a new inspiration is initiated (prop_0) whenever there is a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity, represented by prop_1. This means every time prop_1 occurs, this should lead (possibly immediately) to the initiation of prop_0. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally, whenever prop_1 holds, then eventually prop_0 will hold.

The sentence expresses that a new inspiration (perhaps an event or action) is initiated by either 'prop_1' or 'prop_2', and this event results in 'prop_3'. In temporal terms, whenever 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' becomes true, 'prop_3' must be true at the same step or immediately afterwards. So, globally, every occurrence of 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' is followed by 'prop_3'.
